steps.title: Identify the antecedent
steps.content: The blank possesses narrative, and the thing that has the narrative is the singular epic poem Raghuvaṃśa. We need a singular possessive pronoun with no apostrophe.

steps.title: Apply the rule
steps.content:
Identify the grammar issue: pronoun form and pronoun-antecedent agreement. Rule: use the possessive pronoun "its" (no apostrophe) for singular non-human antecedents; "it's" is a contraction meaning "it is."
  • Choice A — ‘they’re’: contraction of "they are"; plural and not possessive — cannot modify "narrative." ✗
  • Choice B — ‘its’: singular possessive pronoun; correctly refers to the singular poem and shows ownership of the narrative. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘their’: plural possessive pronoun; the poem is singular, so "their" creates a number mismatch. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘it’s’: contraction of "it is"; not possessive, so "it is compelling narrative" is grammatically nonsensical. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
